video_title: Top #DDD Kansas City BBQ Videos with Guy Fieri 🔥 | Diners, Drive-Ins, and Dives | Food Network

restaurants.name: Wood Yard Barbecue
restaurants.guy_intro: To round out this adventure, we're going to visit a place that's supplying a lot of the wood to the barbecue joints in town. And these folks aren't only just doing barbecue wood, they're doing barbecue themselves. This is Wood Yard Barbecue. It really looks more like a place that sells wood. Because that's been the golden ticket at this family business since 1913. The specialty woods, the pecan, the oak, the cherry, the smoking wood. And five years ago, owner Frank Schlegel decided... If the wood's good enough for everybody else, he'd start cooking on it too and add barbecue to the business.

dishes.description: Baby back ribs with a unique dry rub, smoked with pecan wood for three hours, leaving the silver skin on to retain moisture.
dishes.guy_response: Whoa. Those ribs are a knockout. Tons of flavor between the pecan. That is the most neat dry rub I've tried. I wouldn't even put sauce on this. And tender. It's cooked all the fat out of it. That is one of the most righteous rubs I've ever had.

restaurants.name: RJ's Barbecue
restaurants.guy_intro: So here we are. Triple D Kansas City barbecue tour in Mission, Kansas. Now, check out this story. A local dude packs up, moves to New York City to get his culinary creds in a French restaurant and spend 25 years in the high-end joints. Now, in the meantime, he's moonlighting in competition barbecue. Until he realizes, this is what I love, it's time we check out RJ's Barbecue.

dishes.description: Ground pork butt mixed with nutmeg, fresh garlic, salt, cayenne pepper, jalapenos, sugar, and red pepper, wrapped in corn husks and smoked for an hour and a half.
dishes.guy_response: Mmm. It's super tender. It did get the smoke. It went long enough that the jalapenos mellowed out. I'm going to politely tell you that I will be ripping off the corn husk sausage idea. Jalapeno sausage. It's a great combination, the sausage and the jalapeno. It's tremendous. It just crumbles in your mouth.

dishes.description: Denver lamb ribs seasoned with a complex rub including corn dextrose, basil, chili powder, cumin, brown sugar, rosemary, and mustard flour, then smoked for two and a half hours at 250 degrees with the silver skin on.
dishes.guy_response: It's sweet. Oh, man. Tender. Mmm. The rub doesn't overpower it. Sits there and just kind of supports it. You still get to taste the lamb. Mmm. Mmm. Nice smoke ring. I think leaving that silver skin on, which I'm not a big fan of on baby backs, but on this, I really think it did lock in the moisture. Great stuff.

restaurants.name: Smokin' Guns Barbecue
restaurants.guy_intro: Now, I believe that it was about 2010 I came to check this joint out. Husband and wife team won all the major competitions. I mean, they rock it, and they got a funky place where they serve up some bomb burnt ends. Looks like things have changed a little bit, but I know the barbecue hasn't. This is Smokin' Guns Barbecue.

dishes.description: Lightly trimmed brisket seasoned with a championship rub containing salt, dried brown sugar, paprika, chili powder, cumin, cayenne, cinnamon, and cocoa, smoked for 9-11 hours at 225 degrees, then rested and sliced.
dishes.guy_response: Not dry, beautiful. That rub, dude, fantastic. I'd risk life and limb for some more of that.

dishes.description: Brisket point cut, cooked down for two hours after initial smoking, then re-seasoned and smoked for an additional four hours, and cut into chunks.
dishes.guy_response: Holy moly. Holy moly. I've had some burnt ends in my day. They up there somewhere? I'll be in my top three.

dishes.description: Meatloaf made from ground beef, brisket, and pork, seasoned with dried mustard, paprika, thyme, basil, and bread crumbs, with barbecue sauce instead of tomato sauce, then smoked for about four hours.
dishes.guy_response: Delicious. Not dry, super tender. That's good meatloaf. God, that's so good. I want to eat so much more.

restaurants.name: B.B.'s Lawnside Barbecue
restaurants.guy_intro: I'm here in Kansas City, Missouri. Now, most folks, most folks would argue this is like the epicenter of great barbecue. So I'm here to check out a joint that has been smoking the meat and rocking the blues for over 20 years. This G-Squad is today's city boy. This is Clint Boyer, and this is B.B.'s Lawnside Barbecue.

dishes.description: Brisket prepared for burnt ends, seasoned with seasoning salt and meat tenderizer, rubbed generously, and smoked for 12-14 hours in a 1950s hickory smoke pit. The point is then cut and cooked down for two more hours, trimmed, and cut into chunks.
dishes.guy_response: Oh, daddy. That is money. That is money.

restaurants.name: The Pigwich
restaurants.guy_intro: So I'm here in Kansas City, Missouri. You do not roll into this town without calling the head coach of the Kansas City Chiefs, Coach Reed, and saying, listen, buddy, where are we going to eat? We're going to a butcher shop that's also the greatest sandwich shop you've ever eaten at. The real deal. What's it called? The Pigwich.

dishes.description: A sandwich featuring a pulled pork patty (smoked pork butt, ground pork, bacon trimmings) topped with buttermilk-brined, breaded, and fried pork tenderloin, three cheeses (pepper jack, cheddar, provolone), house ranch dressing, andouille spread, house barbecue sauce, and B&B jalapenos on a bun.
dishes.guy_response: Wow. I love the crisp on the pork, and I'm loving the patty. Three cheeses, man, blend perfect. How about the jalapenos? Man, a lot. Bread and butter jalapenos is my new favorite thing. The ranch dressing, you need that cooling, creaming effect. My favorite part of the whole thing, though, is the pulled pork patty. My favorite part is eating it.
